title: AI From Scratch
description: A complete AI engineering curriculum organized into phases, from setup and math foundations through LLMs, agents, infrastructure, safety, and capstone projects.

phases.title: Setup & Tooling
phases.description: Get your environment ready for everything that follows.

lessons.title: Dev Environment
lessons.summary: Your tools shape your thinking. Set them up once, set them up right.

lessons.title: Git & Collaboration
lessons.summary: Version control is not optional. Every experiment, every model, every lesson you build here gets tracked.

lessons.title: GPU Setup & Cloud
lessons.summary: Training on CPU is fine for learning. Training for real needs a GPU.

lessons.title: APIs & Keys
lessons.summary: Every AI API works the same way: send a request, get a response. The details change, the pattern doesn't.

lessons.title: Jupyter Notebooks
lessons.summary: Notebooks are the lab bench of AI engineering. You prototype here, then move what works into production.

lessons.title: Python Environments
lessons.summary: Dependency hell is real. Virtual environments are the cure.

lessons.title: Docker for AI
lessons.summary: Containers make "works on my machine" a thing of the past.

lessons.title: Editor Setup
lessons.summary: Your editor is your co-pilot. Configure it once so it stays out of your way and starts pulling its weight.

lessons.title: Data Management
lessons.summary: Data is the fuel. How you manage it determines how fast you go.

lessons.title: Terminal & Shell
lessons.summary: The terminal is where AI engineers live. Get comfortable here.

lessons.title: Linux for AI
lessons.summary: Most AI runs on Linux. You need to know enough to not be stuck.

lessons.title: Debugging and Profiling
lessons.summary: The worst AI bugs don't crash. They train silently on garbage and report a beautiful loss curve.

phases.title: Math Foundations
phases.description: The intuition behind every AI algorithm, through code — not textbooks.

lessons.title: Linear Algebra Intuition
lessons.summary: Every AI model is just matrix math wearing a fancy hat.

lessons.title: Vectors, Matrices & Operations
lessons.summary: Every neural network is just matrix multiplication with extra steps.

lessons.title: Matrix Transformations
lessons.summary: A matrix is a machine that reshapes space. Learn what it does to every point, and you understand the whole transformation.

lessons.title: Calculus for Machine Learning
lessons.summary: Derivatives tell you which way is downhill. That is all a neural network needs to learn.

lessons.title: Chain Rule & Automatic Differentiation
lessons.summary: The chain rule is the engine behind every neural network that learns.

lessons.title: Probability and Distributions
lessons.summary: Probability is the language AI uses to express uncertainty.

lessons.title: Bayes' Theorem
lessons.summary: Probability is about what you expect. Bayes' theorem is about what you learn.

lessons.title: Optimization
lessons.summary: Training a neural network is nothing more than finding the bottom of a valley.

lessons.title: Information Theory
lessons.summary: Information theory measures surprise. Loss functions are built on it.

lessons.title: Dimensionality Reduction
lessons.summary: High-dimensional data has structure. You find it by looking from the right angle.

lessons.title: Singular Value Decomposition
lessons.summary: SVD is the Swiss Army knife of linear algebra. Every matrix has one. Every data scientist needs one.

lessons.title: Tensor Operations
lessons.summary: Tensors are the common language between data and deep learning. Every image, every sentence, every gradient flows through them.

lessons.title: Numerical Stability
lessons.summary: Floating point is a leaky abstraction. It will bite you during training, and you will not see it coming.

lessons.title: Norms and Distances
lessons.summary: Your distance function defines what "similar" means. Choose wrong and everything downstream breaks.

lessons.title: Statistics for Machine Learning
lessons.summary: Statistics is how you know if your model actually works or just got lucky.

lessons.title: Sampling Methods
lessons.summary: Sampling is how AI explores the space of possibilities.

lessons.title: Linear Systems
lessons.summary: Solving Ax = b is the oldest problem in mathematics that still runs your neural network.

lessons.title: Convex Optimization
lessons.summary: Convex problems have one valley. Neural networks have millions. Knowing the difference matters.

lessons.title: Complex Numbers for AI
lessons.summary: The square root of -1 is not imaginary. It is the key to rotations, frequencies, and half of signal processing.

lessons.title: The Fourier Transform
lessons.summary: Every signal is a sum of sine waves. The Fourier transform tells you which ones.

lessons.title: Graph Theory for Machine Learning
lessons.summary: Graphs are the data structure of relationships. If your data has connections, you need graph theory.

lessons.title: Stochastic Processes
lessons.summary: Randomness with structure. The math behind random walks, Markov chains, and diffusion models.

phases.title: ML Fundamentals
phases.description: Classical machine learning — still the backbone of most production AI.

lessons.title: What Is Machine Learning
lessons.summary: Machine learning is teaching computers to find patterns in data instead of writing rules by hand.

lessons.title: Linear Regression
lessons.summary: Linear regression draws the best straight line through your data. It is the "hello world" of machine learning.

lessons.title: Logistic Regression
lessons.summary: Logistic regression bends a straight line into an S-curve to answer yes-or-no questions with probabilities.

lessons.title: Decision Trees and Random Forests
lessons.summary: A decision tree is just a flowchart. But a forest of them is one of the most powerful tools in ML.

lessons.title: Support Vector Machines
lessons.summary: Find the widest street between two classes. That is the entire idea.

lessons.title: K-Nearest Neighbors and Distances
lessons.summary: Store everything. Predict by looking at your neighbors. The simplest algorithm that actually works.

lessons.title: Unsupervised Learning
lessons.summary: No labels, no teacher. The algorithm finds structure on its own.

lessons.title: Feature Engineering & Selection
lessons.summary: A good feature is worth a thousand data points.

lessons.title: Model Evaluation
lessons.summary: A model is only as good as the way you measure it.

lessons.title: Bias-Variance Tradeoff
lessons.summary: Every model error comes from one of three sources: bias, variance, or noise. You can only control the first two.

lessons.title: Ensemble Methods
lessons.summary: A group of weak learners, combined correctly, becomes a strong learner. This is not a metaphor. It is a theorem.

lessons.title: Hyperparameter Tuning
lessons.summary: Hyperparameters are the knobs you turn before training starts. Turning them well is the difference between a mediocre model and a great one.

lessons.title: ML Pipelines
lessons.summary: A model is not a product. A pipeline is. The pipeline is everything from raw data to deployed prediction, and every step must be reproducible.

lessons.title: Naive Bayes
lessons.summary: The "naive" assumption is wrong, and it works anyway. That's the beauty of it.

lessons.title: Time Series Fundamentals
lessons.summary: Past performance does predict future results -- if you check for stationarity first.

lessons.title: Anomaly Detection
lessons.summary: Normal is easy to define. Abnormal is whatever doesn't fit.

lessons.title: Handling Imbalanced Data
lessons.summary: When 99% of your data is "normal," accuracy is a lie.

lessons.title: Feature Selection
lessons.summary: More features is not better. The right features is better.

phases.title: Deep Learning Core
phases.description: Neural networks from first principles. No frameworks until you build one yourself.

lessons.title: The Perceptron
lessons.summary: The perceptron is the atom of neural networks. Split it open and you find weights, a bias, and a decision.

lessons.title: Multi-Layer Networks and Forward Pass
lessons.summary: One neuron draws a line. Stack them, and you can draw anything.

lessons.title: Backpropagation from Scratch
lessons.summary: Backpropagation is the algorithm that makes learning possible. Without it, neural networks are just expensive random number generators.

lessons.title: Activation Functions
lessons.summary: Without nonlinearity, your 100-layer network is a fancy matrix multiply. Activations are the gates that let neural networks think in curves.

lessons.title: Loss Functions
lessons.summary: Your network makes a prediction. The ground truth says otherwise. How wrong is it? That number is the loss. Pick the wrong loss function and your model optimizes for the wrong thing entirely.

lessons.title: Optimizers
lessons.summary: Gradient descent tells you which direction to move. It says nothing about how far or how fast. SGD is a compass. Adam is GPS with traffic data.

lessons.title: Regularization
lessons.summary: Your model gets 99% on training data and 60% on test data. It memorized instead of learning. Regularization is the tax you impose on complexity to force generalization.

lessons.title: Weight Initialization and Training Stability
lessons.summary: Initialize wrong and training never starts. Initialize right and 50 layers train as smoothly as 3.

lessons.title: Learning Rate Schedules and Warmup
lessons.summary: The learning rate is the single most important hyperparameter. Not the architecture. Not the dataset size. Not the activation function. The learning rate. If you tune nothing else, tune this.

lessons.title: Build Your Own Mini Framework
lessons.summary: You have built neurons, layers, networks, backprop, activations, loss functions, optimizers, regularization, initialization, and LR schedules. All as separate pieces. Now wire them together into a framework. Not PyTorch. Not TensorFlow. Yo...

lessons.title: Introduction to PyTorch
lessons.summary: You built the engine from pistons and crankshafts. Now learn the one everyone actually drives.

lessons.title: Introduction to JAX
lessons.summary: PyTorch mutates tensors. TensorFlow builds graphs. JAX compiles pure functions. That last one changes how you think about deep learning.

lessons.title: Debugging Neural Networks
lessons.summary: Your network compiled. It ran. It produced a number. The number is wrong and nothing crashed. Welcome to the hardest kind of debugging -- the kind where there is no error message.

phases.title: Computer Vision
phases.description: From pixels to understanding — image, video, and 3D.

lessons.title: Image Fundamentals — Pixels, Channels, Color Spaces
lessons.summary: An image is a tensor of light samples. Every vision model you will ever use starts from this one fact.

lessons.title: Convolutions from Scratch
lessons.summary: A convolution is a tiny dense layer you slide across an image, sharing the same weights at every location.

lessons.title: CNNs — LeNet to ResNet
lessons.summary: Every major CNN of the last thirty years is the same conv–nonlinearity–downsample recipe with one new idea bolted on. Learn the ideas in order.

lessons.title: Image Classification
lessons.summary: A classifier is a function from pixels to a probability distribution over classes. Everything else is plumbing.

lessons.title: Transfer Learning & Fine-Tuning
lessons.summary: Somebody else spent a million GPU hours teaching a network what edges, textures, and object parts look like. You should borrow those features before training your own.

lessons.title: Object Detection — YOLO from Scratch
lessons.summary: Detection is classification plus regression, run at every position in a feature map, then cleaned up with non-maximum suppression.

lessons.title: Semantic Segmentation — U-Net
lessons.summary: Segmentation is classification at every pixel. U-Net makes it work by pairing a downsampling encoder with an upsampling decoder and wiring skip connections between them.

lessons.title: Instance Segmentation — Mask R-CNN
lessons.summary: Add a tiny mask branch to a Faster R-CNN detector and you have instance segmentation. The hard part is RoIAlign, and it is harder than it looks.

lessons.title: Image Generation — GANs
lessons.summary: A GAN is two neural networks in a fixed game. One draws, one critiques. They get better together until the drawings fool the critic.

lessons.title: Image Generation — Diffusion Models
lessons.summary: A diffusion model learns to denoise. Train it to remove a tiny bit of noise from a noisy image, repeat that backwards a thousand times, and you have an image generator.

lessons.title: Stable Diffusion — Architecture & Fine-Tuning
lessons.summary: Stable Diffusion is a DDPM that runs in the latent space of a pretrained VAE, conditioned on text via cross-attention, sampled with a fast deterministic ODE solver, and steered by classifier-free guidance.

lessons.title: Video Understanding — Temporal Modeling
lessons.summary: A video is a sequence of images plus the physics that connects them. Every video model either treats time as an extra axis (3D conv), a sequence to attend over (transformer), or a feature to extract once and pool (2D+pool).

lessons.title: 3D Vision — Point Clouds & NeRFs
lessons.summary: 3D vision comes in two flavours. Point clouds are the sensor's raw output. NeRFs are the learned volumetric field. Both answer "what is where in space."

lessons.title: Vision Transformers (ViT)
lessons.summary: Cut the image into patches, treat each patch as a word, run a standard transformer. Don't look back.

lessons.title: Real-Time Vision — Edge Deployment
lessons.summary: Edge inference is the discipline of getting a 90-accuracy model to run at 30 fps on a device with 2 GB of RAM. Every percentage point of accuracy is traded against milliseconds of latency.

lessons.title: Build a Complete Vision Pipeline — Capstone
lessons.summary: A production vision system is a chain of models and rules stitched with data contracts. The pieces are already in this phase; the capstone wires them together end-to-end.

lessons.title: Self-Supervised Vision — SimCLR, DINO, MAE
lessons.summary: Labels are the bottleneck of supervised vision. Self-supervised pretraining removes them: learn visual features from 100M unlabelled images, fine-tune on 10k labelled ones.

lessons.title: Open-Vocabulary Vision — CLIP
lessons.summary: Train an image encoder and a text encoder together so that matching (image, caption) pairs land at the same point in a shared space. That is the whole trick.

lessons.title: OCR & Document Understanding
lessons.summary: OCR is a three-stage pipeline — detect text boxes, recognise the characters, then lay them out. Every modern OCR system reorders these stages or merges them.

lessons.title: Image Retrieval & Metric Learning
lessons.summary: A retrieval system ranks candidates by a distance in embedding space. Metric learning is the discipline of shaping that space so the distances mean what you want.

lessons.title: Keypoint Detection & Pose Estimation
lessons.summary: A pose is a set of ordered keypoints. A keypoint detector is a heatmap regressor. Everything else is bookkeeping.

lessons.title: 3D Gaussian Splatting from Scratch
lessons.summary: A scene is a cloud of millions of 3D Gaussians. Each one has a position, orientation, scale, opacity, and a colour that depends on viewing direction. Rasterise them, backprop through the rasterisation, done.

lessons.title: Diffusion Transformers & Rectified Flow
lessons.summary: The U-Net is not the secret of diffusion. Replace it with a transformer, swap the noise schedule for a straight-line flow, and suddenly you have SD3, FLUX, and every 2026 text-to-image model.

lessons.title: SAM 3 & Open-Vocabulary Segmentation
lessons.summary: Give a model a text prompt and an image and get masks for every matching object. SAM 3 made that a single forward pass.

lessons.title: Vision-Language Models — The ViT-MLP-LLM Pattern
lessons.summary: A vision encoder converts an image into tokens. An MLP projector maps those tokens into the LLM's embedding space. A language model does the rest. That pattern — ViT-MLP-LLM — is every production VLM in 2026.

lessons.title: Monocular Depth & Geometry Estimation
lessons.summary: A depth map is a single-channel image where each pixel is a distance from the camera. Predicting it from one RGB frame used to be impossible without stereo or LiDAR. In 2026 a frozen ViT encoder plus a lightweight head gets within a few pe...

lessons.title: Multi-Object Tracking & Video Memory
lessons.summary: Tracking is detection plus association. Detect every frame. Match this frame's detections to last frame's tracks by ID.

lessons.title: World Models & Video Diffusion
lessons.summary: A video model that predicts the next seconds of a scene is a world simulator. Condition that prediction on actions and you have a learned game engine.

phases.title: NLP — Foundations to Advanced
phases.description: Language is the interface to intelligence. Master every layer.

lessons.title: Text Processing — Tokenization, Stemming, Lemmatization
lessons.summary: Language is continuous. Models are discrete. Preprocessing is the bridge.

lessons.title: Bag of Words, TF-IDF, and Text Representation
lessons.summary: Count first, think later. TF-IDF still beats embeddings on well-defined tasks in 2026.

lessons.title: Word Embeddings — Word2Vec from Scratch
lessons.summary: A word is the company it keeps. Train a shallow net on that idea and geometry falls out.

lessons.title: GloVe, FastText, and Subword Embeddings
lessons.summary: Word2Vec trained one embedding per word. GloVe factorized the co-occurrence matrix. FastText embedded the pieces. BPE bridged to transformers.

lessons.title: Sentiment Analysis
lessons.summary: The canonical NLP task. Most of what you need to know about classical text classification shows up here.

lessons.title: Named Entity Recognition
lessons.summary: Pull the names out. Sounds easy until you deal with ambiguous boundaries, nested entities, and domain jargon.

lessons.title: POS Tagging and Syntactic Parsing
lessons.summary: Grammar was unfashionable for a while. Then every LLM pipeline needed to validate structured extraction, and it came back.

lessons.title: CNNs and RNNs for Text
lessons.summary: Convolutions learn n-grams. Recurrences remember. Both are superseded by attention. Both still matter on constrained hardware.

lessons.title: Sequence-to-Sequence Models
lessons.summary: Two RNNs pretending to be a translator. The bottleneck they hit is the reason attention exists.

lessons.title: Attention Mechanism — The Breakthrough
lessons.summary: The decoder stops squinting at a compressed summary and starts looking at the whole source. Everything after this is attention plus engineering.

lessons.title: Machine Translation
lessons.summary: Translation is the task that paid for NLP research for thirty years and keeps paying now.

lessons.title: Text Summarization
lessons.summary: Extractive systems tell you what the document said. Abstractive systems tell you what the author meant. Different tasks, different pitfalls.

lessons.title: Question Answering Systems
lessons.summary: Three systems shaped modern QA. Extractive found spans. Retrieval-augmented grounded them in documents. Generative produced answers. Every modern AI assistant is a mix of the three.

lessons.title: Information Retrieval and Search
lessons.summary: BM25 is precise but brittle. Dense casts a wide net but misses keywords. Hybrid is the 2026 default. Everything else is tuning.

lessons.title: Topic Modeling — LDA and BERTopic
lessons.summary: LDA: documents are mixtures of topics, topics are distributions over words. BERTopic: documents cluster in embedding space, clusters are topics. Same goal, different decompositions.

lessons.title: Text Generation Before Transformers — N-gram Language Models
lessons.summary: If a word is surprising, the model is bad. Perplexity makes surprise a number. Smoothing keeps it finite.

lessons.title: Chatbots — Rule-Based to Neural to LLM Agents
lessons.summary: ELIZA replied with pattern matches. DialogFlow mapped intents. GPT answered from weights. Claude runs tools and verifies. Each era solved the previous one's worst failure.

lessons.title: Multilingual NLP
lessons.summary: One model, 100+ languages, zero training data for most of them. Cross-lingual transfer is the practical miracle of the 2020s.

lessons.title: Subword Tokenization — BPE, WordPiece, Unigram, SentencePiece
lessons.summary: Word tokenizers choke on unseen words. Character tokenizers blow up sequence length. Subword tokenizers split the difference. Every modern LLM ships on one.

lessons.title: Structured Outputs & Constrained Decoding
lessons.summary: Ask an LLM for JSON. Get JSON most of the time. In production, "most" is the problem. Constrained decoding turns "most" into "always" by editing the logits before sampling.

lessons.title: Natural Language Inference — Textual Entailment
lessons.summary: "t entails h" means a human reading t would conclude h is true. NLI is the task of predicting entailment / contradiction / neutral. Boring on the surface, load-bearing in production.

lessons.title: Embedding Models — The 2026 Deep Dive
lessons.summary: Word2Vec gave you a vector per word. Modern embedding models give you a vector per passage, cross-lingual, with sparse, dense, and multi-vector views, sized to fit your index. Pick wrong and your RAG retrieves the wrong thing.

lessons.title: Chunking Strategies for RAG
lessons.summary: Chunking configuration influences retrieval quality as much as the choice of embedding model (Vectara NAACL 2025). Get chunking wrong and no amount of reranking saves you.

lessons.title: Coreference Resolution
lessons.summary: "She called him. He did not answer. The doctor was at lunch." Three references to two people and nobody is named. Coreference resolution figures out who is who.

lessons.title: Entity Linking & Disambiguation
lessons.summary: NER found "Paris." Entity linking decides: Paris, France? Paris Hilton? Paris, Texas? Paris (the Trojan prince)? Without linking, your knowledge graph stays ambiguous.

lessons.title: Relation Extraction & Knowledge Graph Construction
lessons.summary: NER found the entities. Entity linking anchored them. Relation extraction finds the edges between them. A knowledge graph is the sum of nodes, edges, and their provenance.

lessons.title: LLM Evaluation — RAGAS, DeepEval, G-Eval
lessons.summary: Exact-match and F1 miss semantic equivalence. Human review does not scale. LLM-as-judge is the production answer — with enough calibration to trust the number.

lessons.title: Long-Context Evaluation — NIAH, RULER, LongBench, MRCR
lessons.summary: Gemini 3 Pro advertises 10M tokens of context. At 1M tokens, 8-needle MRCR drops to 26.3%. Advertised ≠ usable. Long-context evaluation tells you the actual capacity of the model you are shipping on.

lessons.title: Dialogue State Tracking
lessons.summary: "I want a cheap restaurant in the north... actually make it moderate... and add Italian." Three turns, three state updates. DST keeps the slot-value dict in sync so the booking works.

phases.title: Speech & Audio
phases.description: The other half of human communication. Hear, understand, speak.

lessons.title: Audio Fundamentals — Waveforms, Sampling, Fourier Transform
lessons.summary: Waveforms are the raw signal. Spectrograms are the representation. Mel features are the ML-friendly form. Every modern ASR and TTS pipeline walks this ladder, and the first rung is understanding sampling and Fourier.

lessons.title: Spectrograms, Mel Scale & Audio Features
lessons.summary: Neural nets do not consume raw waveforms well. They consume spectrograms. They consume mel spectrograms even better. Every ASR, TTS, and audio classifier in 2026 lives or dies by this single preprocessing choice.

lessons.title: Audio Classification — From k-NN on MFCCs to AST and BEATs
lessons.summary: Everything from "dog barking vs siren" to "which language is this" is audio classification. The features are mels. The architecture moves each decade. The evaluation stays AUC, F1, and per-class recall.

lessons.title: Speech Recognition (ASR) — CTC, RNN-T, Attention
lessons.summary: Speech recognition is audio classification at every timestep, glued together by a sequence model that knows English and silence. CTC, RNN-T, and attention are the three ways to do it. Pick one and understand why.

lessons.title: Whisper — Architecture & Fine-Tuning
lessons.summary: Whisper is a 30-second-window transformer encoder-decoder, trained on 680k hours of multilingual weakly-supervised audio-text pairs. One architecture, multiple tasks, robust across 99 languages. The 2026 reference ASR.

lessons.title: Speaker Recognition & Verification
lessons.summary: ASR asks "what did they say?" Speaker recognition asks "who said it?" The math looks the same — embeddings plus cosine — but every production decision hinges on a single EER number.

lessons.title: Text-to-Speech (TTS) — From Tacotron to F5 and Kokoro
lessons.summary: ASR inverts speech to text; TTS inverts text to speech. The 2026 stack is three parts: text → tokens, tokens → mel, mel → waveform. Each part has a default model that fits in a laptop.

lessons.title: Voice Cloning & Voice Conversion
lessons.summary: Voice cloning reads your text in someone else's voice. Voice conversion rewrites your voice into someone else's while preserving what you said. Both hang on the same decomposition: separate speaker identity from content.

lessons.title: Music Generation — MusicGen, Stable Audio, Suno, and the Licensing Earthquake
lessons.summary: 2026 music generation: Suno v5 and Udio v4 dominate commercial; MusicGen, Stable Audio Open, and ACE-Step lead open-source. The technical problem is mostly solved. The legal problem (Warner Music $500M settlement, UMG settlement) reshaped...

lessons.title: Audio-Language Models — Qwen2.5-Omni, Audio Flamingo, GPT-4o Audio
lessons.summary: 2026 audio-language models reason over speech + environmental sound + music. Qwen2.5-Omni-7B matches GPT-4o Audio on MMAU-Pro. Audio Flamingo Next beats Gemini 2.5 Pro on LongAudioBench. The gap between open and closed is essentially close...

lessons.title: Real-Time Audio Processing
lessons.summary: Batch pipelines process a file. Real-time pipelines process the next 20 milliseconds before the next 20 arrive. Every conversational AI, broadcast studio, and telephony bot lives and dies by this latency budget.

lessons.title: Build a Voice Assistant Pipeline — The Phase 6 Capstone
lessons.summary: Everything from lessons 01-11, stitched together. Build a voice assistant that listens, reasons, and talks back. In 2026 that is a solved engineering problem, not a research problem — but the integration details decide whether it ships.

lessons.title: Neural Audio Codecs — EnCodec, SNAC, Mimi, DAC and the Semantic-Acoustic Split
lessons.summary: 2026 audio generation is almost all tokens. EnCodec, SNAC, Mimi, and DAC turn continuous waveforms into discrete sequences that a transformer can predict. The semantic-vs-acoustic token split — first-codebook as semantic, rest as acoustic...

lessons.title: Voice Activity Detection & Turn-Taking — Silero, Cobra, and the Flush Trick
lessons.summary: Every voice agent lives or dies on two decisions: is the user speaking now, and are they done? VAD answers the first. Turn-detection (VAD + silence-hangover + semantic endpoint model) answers the second. Get either wrong and your assistant...

lessons.title: Streaming Speech-to-Speech — Moshi, Hibiki, and Full-Duplex Dialogue
lessons.summary: 2024-2026 redefined voice AI. Moshi ships a single model that listens and speaks simultaneously at 200 ms latency. Hibiki does speech-to-speech translation chunk-by-chunk. Both abandon the ASR → LLM → TTS pipeline for a unified full-duplex...

lessons.title: Voice Anti-Spoofing & Audio Watermarking — ASVspoof 5, AudioSeal, WaveVerify
lessons.summary: Voice cloning shipped faster than defenses. 2026 production voice systems need two things: a detector (AASIST, RawNet2) that classifies real vs fake speech, and a watermark (AudioSeal) that survives compression and editing. Ship both or do...

lessons.title: Audio Evaluation — WER, MOS, UTMOS, MMAU, FAD, and the Open Leaderboards
lessons.summary: You cannot ship what you cannot measure. This lesson names the 2026 metrics for every audio task: ASR (WER, CER, RTFx), TTS (MOS, UTMOS, SECS, WER-on-ASR-round-trip), audio-language (MMAU, LongAudioBench), music (FAD, CLAP), and speaker (E...

phases.title: Transformers Deep Dive
phases.description: The architecture that changed everything. Understand every layer.

lessons.title: Why Transformers — The Problems with RNNs
lessons.summary: RNNs process tokens one at a time. Transformers process all tokens at once. That single architectural bet changed every scaling curve in deep learning after 2017.

lessons.title: Self-Attention from Scratch
lessons.summary: Attention is a lookup table where every word asks "who matters to me?" - and learns the answer.

lessons.title: Multi-Head Attention
lessons.summary: One attention head learns one relation at a time. Eight heads learn eight. Heads are free. Take more of them.

lessons.title: Positional Encoding — Sinusoidal, RoPE, ALiBi
lessons.summary: Attention is permutation-invariant. "The cat sat on the mat" and "mat the on sat cat the" produce the same output without positional signal. Three algorithms fix it — each with a different bet on what "position" means.

lessons.title: The Full Transformer — Encoder + Decoder
lessons.summary: Attention is the star. Everything else — residuals, normalization, feed-forward, cross-attention — is the scaffolding that lets you stack it deep.

lessons.title: BERT — Masked Language Modeling
lessons.summary: GPT predicts the next word. BERT predicts a missing word. One sentence of difference — and half a decade of everything embedding-shaped.

lessons.title: GPT — Causal Language Modeling
lessons.summary: BERT sees both sides. GPT sees only the past. The triangle mask is the most consequential single line of code in modern AI.

lessons.title: T5, BART — Encoder-Decoder Models
lessons.summary: Encoders understand. Decoders generate. Put them back together and you get a model built for input → output tasks: translate, summarize, rewrite, transcribe.

lessons.title: Vision Transformers (ViT)
lessons.summary: An image is a grid of patches. A sentence is a grid of tokens. The same transformer eats both.

lessons.title: Audio Transformers — Whisper Architecture
lessons.summary: Audio is an image of frequency over time. Whisper is a ViT that eats mel spectrograms and speaks back.

lessons.title: Mixture of Experts (MoE)
lessons.summary: A dense 70B transformer activates every parameter for every token. A 671B MoE activates only 37B per token and beats it on every benchmark. Sparsity is the most important scaling idea of the decade.

lessons.title: KV Cache, Flash Attention & Inference Optimization
lessons.summary: Training is parallel and FLOP-bound. Inference is serial and memory-bound. Different bottleneck, different tricks.

lessons.title: Scaling Laws
lessons.summary: The 2020 Kaplan paper said: bigger model, lower loss. The 2022 Hoffmann paper said: you were under-training. Compute goes into two buckets — parameters and tokens — and the split is not obvious.

lessons.title: Build a Transformer from Scratch — The Capstone
lessons.summary: Thirteen lessons. One model. No shortcuts.

lessons.title: Attention Variants — Sliding Window, Sparse, Differential
lessons.summary: Full attention is a circle. Every token sees every token, and memory pays the price. Four variants bend the shape of the circle and recover half the cost.

lessons.title: Speculative Decoding — Draft, Verify, Repeat
lessons.summary: Autoregressive decoding is serial. Each token waits for the previous one. Speculative decoding breaks the chain: a cheap model drafts N tokens, the expensive model verifies all N in one forward pass. When the draft is right you paid one bi...

phases.title: Generative AI
phases.description: Create images, video, audio, 3D, and more.

lessons.title: Generative Models — Taxonomy & History
lessons.summary: Every image model, text model, video model, and 3D model fits in one of five buckets. Pick the wrong bucket and you will fight the math for weeks. Pick the right one and the field's last twelve years of progress stacks cleanly in your head.

lessons.title: Autoencoders & Variational Autoencoders (VAE)
lessons.summary: A plain autoencoder compresses then reconstructs. It memorizes. It does not generate. Add one trick — force the code to look Gaussian — and you get a sampler. That single trick, the reparameterization of z = μ + σ·ε, is why every latent-di...

lessons.title: GANs — Generator vs Discriminator
lessons.summary: Goodfellow's trick in 2014 was to skip density entirely. Two networks. One makes fakes. One catches them. They fight until the fakes are indistinguishable from real. It shouldn't work. It often doesn't. When it does, the samples are still...

lessons.title: Conditional GANs & Pix2Pix
lessons.summary: The first big unlock of 2014-2017 was controlling what a GAN makes. Attach a label, or an image, or a sentence. Pix2Pix did the image version and it still beats every generic text-to-image model on narrow image-to-image tasks.

lessons.title: StyleGAN
lessons.summary: Most generators stir z into every layer at the same time. StyleGAN split it apart: first map z to an intermediate w, then inject w at every resolution level through AdaIN. That single change untangled the latent space and made photorealist...

lessons.title: Diffusion Models — DDPM from Scratch
lessons.summary: Ho, Jain, Abbeel (2020) gave the field a recipe it could not quit. Destroy the data with noise over a thousand small steps. Train one neural net to predict the noise. Reverse the process at inference. Today every mainstream image, video, 3...

lessons.title: Latent Diffusion & Stable Diffusion
lessons.summary: Pixel-space diffusion on 512×512 images is a computational war crime. Rombach et al. (2022) noticed that you do not need all 786k dimensions to generate an image — you need enough to capture semantic structure, and a separate decoder for t...

lessons.title: ControlNet, LoRA & Conditioning
lessons.summary: Text alone is a clumsy control signal. ControlNet lets you clone a pretrained diffusion model and steer it with a depth map, pose skeleton, scribble, or edge image. LoRA lets you fine-tune a 2B-parameter model by training 10 million parame...

lessons.title: Inpainting, Outpainting & Image Editing
lessons.summary: Text-to-image makes new things. Inpainting fixes old ones. In production, 70% of billable image work is editing — swap a background, remove a logo, extend the canvas, regenerate a hand. Inpainting is where diffusion earns its keep.

lessons.title: Video Generation
lessons.summary: An image is a 2-D tensor. A video is a 3-D one. The theory is the same; the compute is 10-100x harder. OpenAI's Sora (Feb 2024) proved it was possible. By 2026 Veo 2, Kling 1.5, Runway Gen-3, Pika 2.0, and WAN 2.2 ship production video fro...

lessons.title: Audio Generation
lessons.summary: Audio is a 1-D signal at 16-48 kHz. A five-second clip is 80-240k samples. No transformer attends to that sequence directly. The solution for every production audio model in 2026 is the same: a neural codec (Encodec, SoundStream, DAC) comp...

lessons.title: 3D Generation
lessons.summary: 3D is the modality where 2D-to-3D leverage is strongest. The 2023 breakthrough was 3D Gaussian Splatting. The 2024-2026 generative push layers multi-view diffusion + 3D reconstruction on top to produce objects and scenes from a single prom...

lessons.title: Flow Matching & Rectified Flows
lessons.summary: Diffusion models take 20-50 sampling steps because they walk a curved path from noise to data. Flow matching (Lipman et al., 2023) and rectified flow (Liu et al., 2022) trained straight paths. Straighter paths mean fewer steps mean faster...

lessons.title: Evaluation — FID, CLIP Score, Human Preference
lessons.summary: Every generative model leaderboard cites FID, CLIP score, and a win rate from a human-preference arena. Each number has a failure mode a determined researcher can game. If you do not know the failure modes, you cannot tell a real improveme...

lessons.title: Visual Autoregressive Modeling (VAR): Next-Scale Prediction
lessons.summary: Diffusion models sample iteratively in time (denoising steps). VAR samples iteratively in scale — it predicts a 1x1 token, then 2x2, then 4x4, up to the final resolution, each scale conditioning on the previous. The 2024 paper showed VAR m...

phases.title: Reinforcement Learning
phases.description: Agents that learn by doing. The foundation of RLHF.

lessons.title: MDPs, States, Actions & Rewards
lessons.summary: A Markov Decision Process is five things: states, actions, transitions, rewards, a discount. Everything in RL — Q-learning, PPO, DPO, GRPO — optimizes over this shape. Learn it once, read the rest of reinforcement learning for free.

lessons.title: Dynamic Programming — Policy Iteration & Value Iteration
lessons.summary: Dynamic programming is RL with cheating. You already know the transition and reward functions; you just iterate the Bellman equation until V or π stops moving. It is the benchmark every sampling-based method tries to approach.

lessons.title: Monte Carlo Methods — Learning from Complete Episodes
lessons.summary: Dynamic programming needs a model. Monte Carlo needs nothing but episodes. Run the policy, watch the returns, average them. The simplest idea in RL — and the one that unlocks everything downstream.

lessons.title: Temporal Difference — Q-Learning & SARSA
lessons.summary: Monte Carlo waits until the episode ends. TD updates after every step by bootstrapping the next value estimate. Q-learning is off-policy and optimistic; SARSA is on-policy and cautious. Both are one line of code. Both underpin every deep-R...

lessons.title: Deep Q-Networks (DQN)
lessons.summary: 2013: Mnih trained one Q-learning network on raw pixels, beat every classical RL agent on seven Atari games. 2015: extended to 49 games, published in Nature, sparked the deep-RL era. DQN is Q-learning plus three tricks that make function a...

lessons.title: Policy Gradient — REINFORCE from Scratch
lessons.summary: Stop estimating value. Parameterize the policy directly, compute the gradient of expected return, step uphill. Williams (1992) wrote it in one theorem. It is why PPO, GRPO, and every LLM RL loop exist.

lessons.title: Actor-Critic — A2C and A3C
lessons.summary: REINFORCE is noisy. Add a critic that learns V̂(s), subtract it from the return, and you get an advantage that has the same expectation but far lower variance. That is actor-critic. A2C runs it synchronously; A3C runs it across threads. Bo...

lessons.title: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO)
lessons.summary: A2C throws away each rollout after one update. PPO wraps the policy gradient in a clipped importance ratio so you can do 10+ epochs on the same data without the policy exploding. Schulman et al. (2017). Still the default policy-gradient al...

lessons.title: Reward Modeling & RLHF
lessons.summary: Humans cannot write a reward function for "good assistant response," but they can compare two responses and pick the better one. Fit a reward model to those comparisons, then RL the language model against it. Christiano 2017. InstructGPT 2...

lessons.title: Multi-Agent RL
lessons.summary: Single-agent RL assumes the environment is stationary. Put two learning agents in the same world and that assumption breaks: each agent is part of the other's environment, and both are changing. Multi-agent RL is the set of tricks to make...

lessons.title: Sim-to-Real Transfer
lessons.summary: A policy trained in a simulator that fails on hardware is a policy that memorized the simulator. Domain randomization, domain adaptation, and system identification are the three tools to make learned controllers cross the reality gap.

lessons.title: RL for Games — AlphaZero, MuZero, and the LLM-Reasoning Era
lessons.summary: 1992: TD-Gammon beat human champions at backgammon with pure TD. 2016: AlphaGo beat Lee Sedol. 2017: AlphaZero dominated chess, shogi, and Go from scratch. 2024: DeepSeek-R1 proved the same recipe, with GRPO replacing PPO, works on reasoni...

phases.description: Build, train, and understand large language models.

lessons.title: Tokenizers: BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece
lessons.summary: Your LLM does not read English. It reads integers. The tokenizer decides whether those integers carry meaning or waste it.

lessons.title: Building a Tokenizer from Scratch
lessons.summary: Lesson 01 gave you a toy. This lesson gives you a weapon.

lessons.title: Data Pipelines for Pre-Training
lessons.summary: The model is a mirror. It reflects whatever data you feed it. Feed it garbage, it reflects garbage with perfect fluency.

lessons.title: Pre-Training a Mini GPT (124M Parameters)

lessons.title: Scaling: Distributed Training, FSDP, DeepSpeed
lessons.summary: Your 124M model trained on one GPU. Now try 7 billion parameters. The model doesn't fit in memory. The data takes weeks on a single machine. Distributed training isn't optional at scale. It's the only path forward.

lessons.title: Instruction Tuning (SFT)
lessons.summary: A base model predicts the next token. That's it. It doesn't follow instructions, answer questions, or refuse harmful requests. SFT is the bridge between a token predictor and a useful assistant. Every model you've ever talked to -- Claude,...

lessons.title: RLHF: Reward Model + PPO

lessons.title: DPO: Direct Preference Optimization

lessons.title: Evaluation: Benchmarks, Evals, LM Harness

lessons.title: Quantization: Making Models Fit
lessons.summary: A 70B model in FP16 needs 140GB. Two A100s just for weights. Quantize to FP8: one 80GB GPU. INT4: a MacBook.

lessons.title: Inference Optimization
lessons.summary: Two phases define LLM inference. Prefill processes your prompt in parallel -- compute-bound. Decode generates tokens one at a time -- memory-bound. Every optimization targets one or both.

lessons.title: Prompt Engineering: Techniques & Patterns

lessons.title: Few-Shot, Chain-of-Thought, Tree-of-Thought
lessons.summary: Telling a model what to do is prompting. Showing it how to think is engineering. The gap between 78% and 91% accuracy on the same model, same task, same data is not a better model. It is a better reasoning strategy.

lessons.title: Structured Outputs: JSON, Schema Validation, Constrained Decoding

lessons.title: Embeddings & Vector Representations

lessons.title: Context Engineering: Windows, Budgets, Memory, and Retrieval

lessons.title: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)

lessons.title: Advanced RAG (Chunking, Reranking, Hybrid Search)

lessons.title: Function Calling & Tool Use

lessons.summary: A ReAct loop written by hand is a while True. A ReAct loop written in LangGraph is a graph you can checkpoint, interrupt, branch, and time-travel through. The agent hasn't changed. The harness around it has.

lessons.title: Agent Workbench Engineering: Why Capable Models Still Fail
lessons.summary: A capable model is not enough. Reliable agents need a workbench: instructions, state, scope, feedback, verification, review, and handoff. Strip those away and even a frontier model produces work that is unsafe to ship.

lessons.title: The Minimal Agent Workbench
lessons.summary: The smallest useful workbench is three files: a root instructions router, a state file, and a task board. Everything else is layered on top. If a repo cannot carry these three, no model will save it.

lessons.title: Agent Instructions as Executable Constraints
lessons.summary: Instructions written as prose are wishes. Instructions written as constraints are tests. The workbench turns each rule into something an agent can check at runtime and a reviewer can verify after the fact.

lessons.title: Repo Memory and Durable State
lessons.summary: Chat history is volatile. The repo is durable. The workbench stores agent state in versioned files so the next session, the next agent, and the next reviewer all read from the same source of truth.

lessons.title: Initialization Scripts for Agents
lessons.summary: Every session that starts cold pays a tax. The agent reads the same files, retries the same probes, and rediscovers the same paths. An init script pays the tax once and writes the answers into state.

lessons.title: Scope Contracts and Task Boundaries
lessons.summary: The model does not know where the work ends. A scope contract is a per-task file that says where the work begins, where it ends, and how to roll back if it spills. The contract turns "stay in scope" from a wish into a check.

lessons.title: Runtime Feedback Loops
lessons.summary: Agents that do not see real command output guess. A feedback runner captures stdout, stderr, exit code, and timing into a structured record the next turn can read. Then the agent reacts to facts instead of to its own prediction of facts.

lessons.title: Verification Gates
lessons.summary: The agent does not get to mark its own work as done. A verification gate reads the scope contract, the feedback log, the rule report, and the diff, and answers a single question: is this task actually complete? If the gate says no, the tas...

lessons.title: Reviewer Agent: Separate Builder from Marker

lessons.title: Multi-Session Handoff
lessons.summary: The session is going to end. The work is not. The handoff packet is the artifact that turns "the agent worked for an hour" into "the next session is productive in the first minute." Build it on purpose, not as an afterthought.

lessons.title: The Workbench on a Real Repo
lessons.summary: Eleven lessons of surfaces are worth nothing if they do not survive contact with a real codebase. This lesson runs the same task twice on a small sample app: prompt-only versus workbench-guided. The numbers do the arguing.

lessons.title: Capstone: Ship a Reusable Agent Workbench Pack
lessons.summary: The mini-track ends with a pack you drop into any repo. Eleven lessons of surfaces compressed into a directory you can cp -r and have an agent working reliably the next morning. The capstone is the artifact this curriculum trades on.

lessons.summary: A pretraining run is a function from token ids to gradients. This lesson builds the conveyor that feeds the ids in.

lessons.title: Token and Positional Embeddings
lessons.summary: Ids are integers. The model wants vectors. Two lookup tables sit between them, and the choice of the positional one shapes what the model can learn.

lessons.title: Multi-Head Self-Attention
lessons.summary: One linear projection, three views, H parallel heads, one mask. The attention block as the model actually uses it.

lessons.title: Transformer Block from Scratch
lessons.summary: One block is the unit of every modern decoder LLM. Layer norm, multi head attention, residual, MLP, residual. The pre-LN variant trains stably without warmup. The post-LN variant is what the original paper shipped. This lesson builds both,...

lessons.title: GPT Model Assembly
lessons.summary: Twelve blocks stacked, a token embedding, a learned position embedding, a final LayerNorm, and a tied language model head. That is the entire 124 million parameter GPT model. This lesson assembles those pieces into a working class, counts...

lessons.title: Training Loop and Evaluation
lessons.summary: A loop that does not measure is a loop that lies. This lesson builds the training loop that drives the GPT model: AdamW with weight decay split, a warmup plus cosine learning rate schedule, a calc_loss_batch helper, an evaluate_model pass...

lessons.title: Loading Pretrained Weights
lessons.summary: Training a 124 million parameter model from scratch is a budget decision; loading a published checkpoint is a Tuesday. This lesson loads pretrained GPT-2 style weights from a safetensors file into the exact architecture from lesson 35, wal...

lessons.title: Capstone Lesson 38: Classifier Fine-Tuning by Head Swap
lessons.summary: Track B's first capstone. A pretrained language model is a stack of self-attention blocks ending in a token-prediction head. When you want spam vs ham, the head is wrong but the body is mostly right. This lesson rips the head off, glues a...

lessons.title: Capstone Lesson 39: Instruction Tuning by Supervised Fine-Tuning
lessons.summary: A pretrained base model can extend a sequence but cannot follow an instruction. Supervised fine-tuning is the smallest change that fixes this: feed the model paired examples of an instruction and a desired response, and train the body to p...

lessons.title: Capstone Lesson 40: Direct Preference Optimization from Scratch
lessons.summary: Reward models and PPO are the classical RLHF stack. DPO collapses that stack into a single supervised loss that fits a policy directly against preference pairs. This lesson derives the DPO loss from the reward-difference identity, ships a...

lessons.title: Capstone Lesson 41: Full Evaluation Pipeline
lessons.summary: Training is the part you can monitor with loss curves. Evaluation is the part you have to design. This lesson builds a unified eval pipeline that takes any trained language model, runs four heterogeneous evals on it, aggregates the results...

lessons.title: Large Corpus Downloader
lessons.summary: Training a language model begins long before the first forward pass. The corpus has to land on disk, decompressed, deduplicated, and addressable, with the resume story already worked out before the network drops at 4 percent. This lesson b...

lessons.title: HDF5 Tokenized Corpus
lessons.summary: The downloaded corpus has to land in a layout the trainer can stream from at line speed. JSONL on disk does not survive 16 dataloader workers. HDF5 with a resizable, chunked integer dataset does. This lesson builds streaming tokenization i...

lessons.title: Cosine LR with Linear Warmup
lessons.summary: The learning-rate schedule is the second most important decision after the loss function. AdamW with a cosine decay and a linear warmup is the modern default for language-model training because it lets the model see a small effective step...

lessons.title: Gradient Clipping and Mixed Precision
lessons.summary: The optimizer and schedule from the previous lesson assume gradients are sane. They usually are not. A single bad batch can spike the gradient norm by three orders of magnitude. Mixed-precision training amplifies this by introducing FP16 o...

lessons.title: Gradient Accumulation
lessons.summary: Train at an effective batch you cannot afford, one micro-batch at a time. Scale the loss, hold the optimizer step, and let the gradients pile up.

lessons.title: Checkpoint Save and Resume
lessons.summary: Train interrupts kill runs; checkpoints let them continue. Save model, optimizer, scheduler, loss history, step counter, and RNG state, atomically, so a kill at any moment leaves a valid file on disk.

lessons.title: Distributed Data Parallel and FSDP from Scratch
lessons.summary: Multi-rank training is two collectives and one rule. Broadcast the parameters at startup, average the gradients after backward, never let the ranks disagree about what step they are on.

lessons.title: Language Model Evaluation Harness
lessons.summary: A model that does well on a task you cannot define is a model that does well by accident. The harness is the task definition, the metric, the runner, and the leaderboard, in one short, swappable shape.

lessons.title: Hypothesis Generator
lessons.summary: A research agent that asks the same question twice is wasting tokens. The trick is forcing each draft to land somewhere new.

lessons.title: Literature Retrieval
lessons.summary: A hypothesis is cheap. Knowing whether someone already proved it is the expensive part. Build the retrieval layer that answers that question before the runner spins up a sandbox.

lessons.title: Experiment Runner
lessons.summary: The loop is only as honest as its measurements. Build the runner that takes a spec, executes it in a sandboxed subprocess, and emits a json metrics blob the evaluator can trust.

lessons.title: Result Evaluator
lessons.summary: The runner produced numbers. The evaluator decides whether those numbers are an improvement, a regression, or noise. Build the verdict path that turns metrics into a one line conclusion.

lessons.title: Paper Writer
lessons.summary: A LaTeX skeleton is a contract between the researcher and the typesetter. If the contract is broken the document does not compile, and the failure is loud. Build the skeleton first, then fill it.

lessons.title: Critic Loop
lessons.summary: A critic that returns "looks good" the first time is broken. A critic that always returns "needs work" is broken. The interesting critic is the one that converges, and you have to engineer convergence.

lessons.title: Iteration Scheduler
lessons.summary: A research loop without a scheduler is a queue with delusions. The scheduler is where the loop decides what to stop exploring, and that decision is the whole game.

lessons.title: End-to-End Research Demo
lessons.summary: A demo is the place where every contract you wrote earlier has to compose. If any one of them leaks, the demo is the lesson that catches it.

lessons.title: Vision Encoder Patches
lessons.summary: A vision model that reads pixels needs a tokenizer for pixels. Patch embedding is that tokenizer. Cut the image into a grid of squares, flatten each square, project it through one linear layer, then add a 2D position signal so the transfor...

lessons.title: Vision Transformer Encoder
lessons.summary: Patches alone do not see. A 12-layer pre-LN transformer with 12 attention heads turns the sequence of patch tokens into a sequence of contextual tokens, with the CLS token pooling whole-image features in its final hidden state. This lesson...

lessons.title: Projection Layer for Modality Alignment
lessons.summary: A vision encoder produces image tokens. A text decoder consumes text tokens. The two live in different vector spaces. A small two-layer MLP projects image tokens into the text embedding space, and a cosine alignment loss against a paired c...

lessons.title: Cross-Attention Fusion
lessons.summary: The projection layer aligns one image vector with one caption vector. A real vision-language decoder needs every text token to attend to every patch token, so the model can ground each word in a region. Cross-attention is how that groundin...

lessons.title: Vision-Language Pretraining
lessons.summary: The encoder, projection, and decoder are wired. Now train them together. Two objectives drive learning: a contrastive image-text loss (InfoNCE) that pulls matching pairs together in the joint embedding space, and a language modeling loss t...

lessons.title: Multimodal Evaluation
lessons.summary: Training is half the loop. The other half is measurement. This lesson builds three evaluation surfaces from primitives: image-caption retrieval reported as R@1, R@5, R@10; visual question answering reported as exact match accuracy; and ima...

lessons.title: Chunking Strategies, Compared
lessons.summary: Chunking decides what your retriever can ever surface. Get the boundaries wrong and no embedding model, no reranker, no LLM can repair the damage downstream.

lessons.title: Hybrid Retrieval with BM25 and Dense Embeddings
lessons.summary: Lexical and semantic retrieval fail on opposite query distributions. Hybrid retrieval with reciprocal rank fusion does not interpolate, it votes - and the vote wins on every query class.

lessons.title: Cross-Encoder Reranker
lessons.summary: A bi-encoder embeds query and document independently. A cross-encoder concatenates them and reads both at once. The cross-encoder is the smartest reader and the slowest. Used as a second stage on the bi-encoder's top-k, it pays for itself.

lessons.title: Query Rewriting: HyDE, Multi-Query, and Decomposition
lessons.summary: The query the user types is not the query your retriever wants. Rewriting bridges the gap before retrieval, so the index sees something closer to what the answer looks like.

lessons.title: RAG Evaluation: Precision, Recall, MRR, nDCG, Faithfulness, Answer Relevance
lessons.summary: If you cannot grade your retrieval and your answer at the same time, you cannot ship the system. The two are not the same metric and the same prompt fails on different axes.

lessons.title: End-to-End RAG System
lessons.summary: Six lessons of components. One pipeline. One eval loop. One self-terminating demo. This is the system you ship.

lessons.title: Task Spec Format
lessons.summary: An eval harness is only as good as the contract its tasks honour. Freeze the JSONL shape and the metric vocabulary before you write a single scoring function.

lessons.title: Classical Metrics
lessons.summary: BLEU, ROUGE-L, F1, exact-match, accuracy. Five metrics that still account for most published LLM eval numbers. Implement each from first principles so you know what the number means.

lessons.title: Code Exec Metric
lessons.summary: Generated code is right when it passes the tests. The eval harness has to extract code, run it without crashing the host, and tally pass-rates honestly. This lesson builds that surface.

lessons.title: Perplexity and Calibration
lessons.summary: If your model says 90 percent confident on a thousand answers and gets six hundred right, it is not well calibrated. Calibration is half of trustworthy eval. The other half is perplexity, which tells you whether the model thinks the held-o...

lessons.title: Leaderboard Aggregation
lessons.summary: Per-task scores are easy. Per-model rankings across heterogeneous tasks are harder. Statistical significance on a thousand-prediction leaderboard is the part everyone skips. This lesson does not skip it.

lessons.title: End-to-End Eval Runner
lessons.summary: Five lessons of plumbing, one lesson to glue them. The runner reads the task spec from lesson 70, calls a model through an adapter, scores with lessons 71 and 72, attaches the calibration report from lesson 73, and emits the leaderboard fr...

lessons.title: Collective Ops From Scratch
lessons.summary: The four collective operations that hold distributed training together are allreduce, broadcast, allgather, and reduce_scatter. Every other primitive a training framework offers is a wrapper around these. Build them once over a multiproces...

lessons.title: Data Parallel DDP From Scratch
lessons.summary: DistributedDataParallel is a hook on top of allreduce. Wrap a model, broadcast the initial parameters from rank 0 so every rank starts identical, install a backward hook on every parameter that issues an allreduce of the gradient, and the...

lessons.title: ZeRO Optimizer State Sharding
lessons.summary: Adam stores two moment estimates per parameter, both in float32. A 7B-parameter model carries 56 GB of optimiser state. ZeRO stage 1 shards that across N ranks; each rank owns 1/N of the optimiser. After the local step the updated paramete...

lessons.title: Pipeline Parallel and Bubble Analysis
lessons.summary: Tensor parallelism splits the matrix multiply across ranks. Pipeline parallelism splits the model across ranks, one stage per rank. Microbatches flow through the pipeline. The empty time at the start and end is the bubble; minimising it is...

lessons.title: Sharded Checkpoint and Atomic Resume
lessons.summary: A 70B-parameter training job is paused by a node failure every few hours. The checkpoint format decides whether you lose 30 minutes or 30 hours. A sharded checkpoint writes every rank's shard in parallel and records ownership in a manifest...

lessons.title: End-to-End Distributed Training
lessons.summary: Lessons 76 through 80 each built one piece. This is the assembly: a tiny GPT trained across 4 simulated ranks with DDP for gradient sync, ZeRO-1 for optimiser-state sharding, and a sharded checkpoint at the halfway mark. The demo runs 20 s...

lessons.title: Capstone 82 — Jailbreak Taxonomy
lessons.summary: A safety harness without a taxonomy is a coin flip. Name the attack before you defend it.

lessons.title: Capstone 83 — Prompt Injection Detector
lessons.summary: A detector is a function from prompt to confidence and category. Anything else is a vibe.

lessons.title: Capstone 84 — Refusal Evaluation
lessons.summary: Helpfulness on benign prompts and refusal on harmful prompts are two metrics, not one. Measure both.

lessons.title: Capstone 85 — Content Classifier Integration
lessons.summary: Classifiers on the output side answer a different question than rules on the input side. Both need a policy router.

lessons.title: Capstone 86 — Constitutional Rules Engine
lessons.summary: A rule is a name, a predicate, and an explanation. Anything missing one of those three is a vibe, not a rule.

lessons.title: Capstone 87 — End-to-End Safety Gate
lessons.summary: Pre-gen, during-gen, post-gen. Three checkpoints, one verdict, an audit trail per request.
